research_sources: Pragmatic and Linguistic Etiquette of Greeting in Gikuyu Language Speakers in Kenya - Nancy I. Karia-Maina (2014)
Field data from Kiambu and Nyeri regions

cultural_context.primary_principle: Greetings are essential oil for peaceful social relations
cultural_context.greeting_obligation: Expected to greet everyone met, whether known or unknown (traditional)
cultural_context.modern_changes: Urban youth influenced by Western culture may only greet close acquaintances
cultural_context.social_function: Establish interpersonal relationships, introduce business/social topics, maintain harmony

notes: Kikuyu does not have gender-defining pronouns (he/she)
Ní is used for emphasis and often omitted in spoken form
Vowel sounds: a(arm), e(egg), i(in), o(opposite), u(ululation), í(it), ú(own/oak)
Greetings are essential for establishing interpersonal relationships
Deference to age, sex, context and time are important factors
Longer greetings = more polite and courteous
Response should be equal or more courteous than offered greeting

initiation_rules: Smaller group greets larger group first
Walking person greets sitting person first
greeting_hierarchy.response_rule: Greetee should respond with equal or more courteous greeting
greeting_hierarchy.politeness_principle: The longer the greeting term, the more polite and courteous it is

intents.cultural_significance: Greeting of Gikuyu (founder of tribe) and offspring. Forefathers ordered Gikuyus to spread this greeting. Most polite and commonly used on any social occasion.
intents.usage_notes: Independent of time and context. Can be used any time of day, any social occasion. Shows respect, cultural loyalty, adherence to tradition.

responses.translation: Peace be upon you! Peace, peace!
responses.notes: Complete courteous version - conforms to tradition of responding with more courteous greeting
responses.usage_context: Most respectful response, used with equals or superiors

responses.notes: Shows concern for the other person

etiquette_rules: Longer greeting is more polite and courteous
Response should be at least equal in courtesy to offered greeting
Shows speaker's knowledge of traditions and culture
Syntactically, males use same term for individuals or groups, males or females
Using this greeting shows loyalty to Gikuyu culture
intents.teaching_notes: This is the first greeting children learn. Instilled from childhood as socio-religious greeting.

intents.cultural_significance: Less polite than Thayu but signals more intimacy and solidarity. Neutral term, not time-specific.
intents.usage_notes: Can be used during day or night, on any social occasion. More intimate and less formal than Thayu.

gender_usage.notes: Women seem to use this more than men

responses.translation: I am fine! And you, how are you?
responses.literal_meaning: I am good! And you, you are how?

responses.translation: I am very well, thank you!

responses.notes: Adding 'nikwega nikwega' shows respect and warmth of welcome

responses.literal_meaning: I am good! You are good?

inappropriate_responses.notes: Sometimes used by females but violates etiquette rules - too brief and less courteous
inappropriate_responses.why_inappropriate: Does not reciprocate with equal courtesy greeting

intents.cultural_significance: Originally meant 'are you fine' but now used only to mean 'hello'. More intimate and less formal than Thayu.
intents.usage_notes: Neutral and context-free. Used any time and any social occasion. Semantic shift from question to greeting.

semantic_evolution.notes: Semantic point: although originally a question, now only used as greeting

responses.translation: Hello! You have come to people who are like your folk and to a place that is smooth.
responses.notes: Complete courteous version - shows respect and warmth of welcome

responses.translation: Hello! How are you? / What's your news?

non_verbal_requirement.facial_expression: Greeting without smile or appropriate mode may reveal hostility or lack of intimacy
non_verbal_requirement.importance: Non-verbal mode conveys pragmatic and sociocultural aspects of meaning

intents.youth_overgeneralization: Young people may use 'nikwega' inappropriately on all occasions, criticized by adults for social mistakes

intents.cultural_significance: Time-dependent greeting. Age and sex marker - used more by females and elderly.
intents.usage_notes: Less common than Thayu or Nikwega. Old females use more courteous variants most frequently.

gender_usage.notes: Females tend to use time-specific greetings more than males

age_usage.elderly_female: very_high_frequency - often use most courteous variants

intents.social_marker: Time-specific greetings may be age and sex markers

responses.translation: Good morning! How are you today?
responses.literal_meaning: How are you morning! You are how today?
responses.notes: Courteous variant, inquires about today specifically

responses.literal_meaning: News of morning! I am good.

responses.text: Úhoro wa rúciní! Gía na múthenya mwega.
responses.translation: Good morning! Have a good day.
responses.literal_meaning: News of morning! Go with day good.
How are you morning! You are good?

typical_responses_from_paper: Úhoro wa rúciní (Good morning - literally 'news of morning')

examples.bot_response: Úhoro wa rúciní! Gía na múthenya mwega.

intents.cultural_significance: Time-dependent greeting. Less common than Thayu or Nikwega.
intents.usage_notes: Used for both afternoon (míaraho) and evening (húainí). More used by females.

responses.literal_meaning: News of evening! You are how?

responses.text: Úhoro wa húainí! Ndí mwega mũno.
responses.translation: Good afternoon/evening! I am very well.
responses.literal_meaning: News of evening! I am good very.

responses.text: Úhoro wa húainí! Gúa na húainí hega.
responses.translation: Good evening! Have a good evening.
responses.literal_meaning: News of evening! Pass with evening good.

examples.bot_response: Úhoro wa húainí! Gúa na húainí hega.

intents.cultural_significance: Context-specific: used when large number of sitting people and no need to shake everyone's hand. Age and sex-marked.
intents.usage_notes: Used mostly by adults greeting younger people, NOT vice versa. Originally for physical laborers, now widespread.
inappropriate_contexts: When handshaking is expected (may signal relationship no longer close)

age_restriction.notes: Used mostly by adults greeting younger people, not vice versa

semantic_evolution.current_usage: Used to greet anyone, even sitting people

responses.translation: We are well! And you, how are you?
responses.literal_meaning: We are good! And you, you are how?

responses.translation: We are well! Are you well?
responses.literal_meaning: We are good! You are good?

etiquette_rules: Inappropriate if handshaking is necessary
Not using handshake with this greeting may indicate relationship is no longer close
Should determine verbal and non-verbal rules before use
Culturally used when large number of sitting greetees
intents.youth_mistakes: Young people may use it and shake hands as well - usually criticized by adults for committing social mistake

intents.cultural_significance: Originally for greeting someone performing physical job. Now becoming widespread for general use.
intents.usage_notes: Can be used to greet anyone now, even sitting people. Casual and friendly.

semantic_evolution.original_meaning: say something / what's the news (to worker)
semantic_evolution.original_context: Greeting someone performing physical job requiring effort
semantic_evolution.current_usage: Used to greet anyone, even sitting people

responses.translation: I am fine! Are you well?
responses.literal_meaning: I am good! You are good?

intents.cultural_significance: Greetings normally followed by inquiries about interlocutors' health. More formal and courteous.
intents.usage_notes: Used to establish social contact. Greeter may not really wish to know details, but does so in compliance with etiquette rules.

responses.text: Nĩ mwega mũno! Ciana cĩaku ithii atía?
responses.translation: Very well! How are your children?
responses.literal_meaning: Is good very! Children yours go how?
responses.notes: Reciprocates by inquiring about family - establishing social contact

responses.text: Tũrĩ ega ithuothe. Nawe nacio cianyu?
responses.translation: We are all well. And yours too?
responses.literal_meaning: We are good all. And you and children yours?
responses.notes: Inquires about each other's children and relatives to establish social contact

intents.combined_usage: Some people may use both 'ukuuga atía' and 'uga', beginning with the former then the latter

examples.bot_response: Nĩ mwega mũno! Ciana cĩaku ithii atía?

intents.cultural_significance: Less formal than 'Ukuuga atía'. Used between intimate conversants or by superiors to subordinates.
intents.usage_notes: Signals intimacy. Sometimes used by superiors to ask about subordinates' health.

intents.cultural_significance: Shows respect, urbanity, and that speaker is cultured and polite. Used when level of intimacy is low.
intents.usage_notes: Greetings associated with proper term of address are more polite than those without. Signals respect and formality.
intents.social_function: Shows speaker is knowledgeable of linguistic and social norms - seen as educated and civilized

responses.text: Nikwega! Ndí mwega mũno. Wĩtwa atía?
responses.translation: Hello! I am very well. What is your name?
responses.literal_meaning: Welcome! I am good very. You are called what?
responses.notes: Professional, inquires about name to personalize

responses.text: Niatia! Ndí mwega. Nĩ kĩĩ kĩngĩkũteithia?
responses.translation: Hello! I am fine. How can I help you?
responses.literal_meaning: How are you! I am good. Is what can-I-help-you?

responses.translation: Hello! I am pleased to see you.

etiquette_rules: Shows speaker is knowledgeable of linguistic and social norms
Signals respect and formality between interactants
Considered more polite than greetings without titles
Used among equal, unintimate people to show respect
Absolute forms of address used when level of intimacy is low

professional_titles.miss: miss (borrowed, used by educated people to avoid discourteous 'kairitu')

examples.bot_response: Nikwega! Ndí mwega mũno. Wĩtwa atía?
Niatia! Ndí mwega. Nĩ kĩĩ kĩngĩkũteithia?

intents.cultural_significance: Used to show solidarity and increase intimacy. Gikuyus extend social titles beyond actual family to display courtesy or solidarity.
intents.usage_notes: When level of rapport and intimacy are relatively high. Can be used by elders to younger people to show solidarity.

generalization_for_solidarity.description: Gikuyus often extend social titles beyond actual family
generalization_for_solidarity.purpose: Display courtesy to or solidarity with recipient
examples: Old people use 'wakia iyu/awa' (mother/father) to younger people to show solidarity and increase intimacy
Younger use 'wakia maitu/baba' to old people of parents' age
Use 'wakia cucu/guka' to greet grandparents or elderly

gender_usage.middle_aged_women: Use kinship greetings much more than middle-aged men
gender_usage.notes: To increase solidarity, middle-aged women use greetings associated with kinship terms more frequently

responses.translation: Hello my child! Are you well?
responses.literal_meaning: Welcome child mine! You are good?
responses.notes: Elder to younger response - affectionate, paternal

responses.literal_meaning: How are you! I am good very.

responses.text: Niatia mwana wa maitũ! Ũkũ wa kũ?
responses.translation: Hello child of my mother! Where are you coming from?
responses.literal_meaning: How are you child of mother-our! You-come from where?

immediate_family.maitu: mother / respected woman of mother's age
immediate_family.awa/baba: father / respected man of father's age

extended_family.wanyua: paternal uncle / respected man (used even when greetee is superior in age)
extended_family.gacucu: grandson (inverse - grandmother can also be called this by grandchild)

general_kinship.mukuru: old person / elder (can signal intimacy if used appropriately)

inverse_terms.cucu/gacucu: Can be used by both grandmother and grandchild to greet each other - inverse term of address
inverse_terms.usage_note: Shows reciprocal respect and affection across generations

etiquette_rules: Shows solidarity and intimacy
Can be used beyond actual family relationships
Middle-aged women use kinship greetings more than middle-aged men
Increases warmth and social bonding
Relational forms used when level of rapport and intimacy are relatively high
Extended social titles display courtesy to or solidarity with recipient

examples.context: Child greeting father or respected elder
Grandchild greeting grandmother or vice versa

examples.bot_response: Niatia mwana wa maitũ! Ũkũ wa kũ?
examples.context: Greeting uncle or respected elder man

intents.cultural_significance: Critical for social harmony and displaying good upbringing. Shows proper respect to elders.
intents.usage_notes: Always use respectful terms and honorifics with elders. May include kinship terms even with non-relatives.

responses.translation: Hello my child! Are you well?
responses.literal_meaning: How are you child mine! You are good?
responses.notes: Elders often use high-pitched voice with equals or superiors, low-pitched with younger

responses.translation: Hello! Where are you coming from?
responses.notes: Shows genuine interest in younger person's activities

responses.text: Wĩ mwega mwana wakwa! Thiĩ na wega.
responses.translation: You are well my child! Go well.
responses.literal_meaning: You are good child mine! Go with goodness.
responses.notes: Blessing-like farewell, shows elder's affection and care

responses.translation: My daughter/son! I am fine. And you?
responses.literal_meaning: Mine mother! I am good. And you?
responses.notes: Old males often use 'wakwa maitu or wanyua' more than others

handshaking.old_males_to_young: May use one hand, shorter duration

voice_characteristics.importance: Paralinguistic components reveal age and knowledge about cultural greetings

body_language.greeting_duration: Old males prolong greetings when greeting equals or superiors

preferred_terms: Wakwa maitu/wanyua (how are you my daughter/son)
characteristics: Use more courteous, longer greeting terms
Use low-pitched voice with younger people

examples.bot_response: Wĩ mwega mwana wakwa! Thiĩ na wega.

intents.cultural_significance: Can be discourteous depending on age/status relationship. Generally used by superiors or among equals.
intents.usage_notes: Rarely directed from inferiors to superiors. May be considered impolite in many contexts.
inappropriate_contexts: Younger person to older person (without permission)

social_restrictions.direction: Used by superiors or among equals, rarely from inferiors to superiors
social_restrictions.age_factor: It is effrontery for younger person to call older by first name without being asked to do so
social_restrictions.character_judgment: Only a very underbred, thick-skinned person would attempt it (Post, 1992:54)

responses.literal_meaning: How are you! I am good.
responses.usage: Among equals or subordinate to superior

etiquette_warnings: Shows lack of proper upbringing if used inappropriately
May damage social relationships if used by younger to older
Should only be used when invited or among close equals

examples.context: Superior greeting subordinate or peers greeting
examples.appropriate: If speaker is elder or peer

related_discourteous_terms.usage: Sometimes used by old men to 30-year-old females whose names they don't know
related_discourteous_terms.problem: Lack of suitable terms for unacquainted adult females

related_discourteous_terms.usage: Used by elders to young people

related_discourteous_terms.issue: Can be discourteous with unacquainted female
related_discourteous_terms.acceptable_usage: Signals intimacy between conversants if used appropriately
related_discourteous_terms.problem: If used with unacquainted person, considered discourteous

alternatives_for_adult_females: madaam (madam) - borrowed term used by educated people
intents.cultural_note: The normal terms for addressing females are 'kairitu' (girl - discourteous) and 'mutumia' (woman - husband-specific, used only by some uncivilized husbands). Educated people avoid both and use borrowed English terms.

intents.cultural_significance: Adding 'nikwega nikwega' to any greeting shows respect and warmth of welcome.
intents.usage_notes: Can be added to any greeting to increase warmth and show genuine welcome. Shows hospitality.
appropriate_contexts: Showing genuine happiness to see someone
intents.augmentation_function: Gikuyus often add 'nikwega nikwega' to any greeting in order to show respect and warmth of welcome

responses.literal_meaning: Welcome welcome! I am good very!

responses.translation: I am happy to see you!
That's good! I'm glad to hear that.
Very good! What can I help you with?

responses.text: Ní wega! Gía na múthenya mwega.
responses.translation: That's good! Have a good day.
responses.literal_meaning: Is good! Go with day good.

responses.text: Nĩ wega mũno! Thiĩ na wega.
responses.literal_meaning: Is good very! Go with goodness.

responses.text: Níndakena ní gúkuona o na niĩ!

examples.bot_response: Níndakena ní gúkuona o na niĩ!

responses.literal_meaning: You are good! You are there!

responses.text: Nĩ wega! Kaĩ ũngĩenda kĩndũ kĩngĩ?
responses.translation: You're welcome! Do you need anything else?
responses.literal_meaning: Is good! Question you-want thing other?

examples.bot_response: Nĩ wega! Kaĩ ũngĩenda kĩndũ kĩngĩ?

intents.cultural_significance: Different farewells for person leaving vs person staying. Shows understanding of social context.
intents.usage_notes: Use 'Thiĩ na wega' (go well) to person leaving, 'Tigwo na wega' (stay well) to person staying.

responses.notes: Person staying says this to person leaving
Person leaving says this to person staying

responses.text: Thiĩ na wega! Gía na múthenya mwega.
responses.translation: Go well! Have a good day.
responses.literal_meaning: Go with goodness! Go with day good.

responses.text: Tuonane ica ikuhí! Thiĩ na wega.
responses.literal_meaning: Let-us-see-each-other soon near! Go with goodness.

responses.text: Ĩĩ, tuonane o ica! Tigwo na wega.
responses.translation: Yes, see you soon! Stay well.
responses.literal_meaning: Yes, let-us-see-each-other just soon! Stay with goodness.

responses.translation: See you! Have a good day.

examples.bot_response: Tuonane ica ikuhí! Thiĩ na wega.
Ĩĩ, tuonane o ica! Tigwo na wega.

responses.text: Ngatho! Nawe gía na múthenya mwega!
responses.translation: Thank you! You too have a good day!
responses.literal_meaning: Thanks! And you go with day good!

responses.text: Nĩ niĩ ndakena! Nawe ũgũo noguo!
responses.literal_meaning: Is me I-am-happy! And you that so-too!

responses.text: Wĩ mwega! Nawe thiĩ na wega!
responses.translation: Thank you! You go well too!
responses.literal_meaning: You are good! And you go with goodness!

examples.bot_response: Ngatho! Nawe gía na múthenya mwega!
Wĩ mwega! Nawe thiĩ na wega!

responses.translation: Sleep well! See you in the morning.

responses.text: Tigwo na wega! Úhoro wa ũtukũ.
responses.literal_meaning: Stay with goodness! News of night.

responses.text: Ĩĩ, nĩ wega! Nĩ kĩĩ kĩngĩ?
responses.literal_meaning: Yes, is good! Is what other?

responses.text: Nĩ wega mũno! Thiiaga na mbere.
responses.literal_meaning: Is good very! Go-continue with front.

examples.bot_response: Ĩĩ, nĩ wega! Nĩ kĩĩ kĩngĩ?
Nĩ wega mũno! Thiiaga na mbere.

responses.text: Nĩ wega, ndĩkúigua. Nĩ kĩĩ kĩngĩenda?
responses.translation: Okay, I understand. What do you want?
responses.literal_meaning: Is good, I-you-understand. Is what you-want?

responses.translation: Yes, I hear you. Another way?

examples.bot_response: Nĩ wega, ndĩkúigua. Nĩ kĩĩ kĩngĩenda?

common_words.tuonane: see you / let us see each other

professional_titles.miss: miss (borrowed English, used for adult females)

pronunciation_guide.tonal_note: Kikuyu is a tonal language. Accent marks (í, ú) indicate high tones which affect meaning.

elderly_characteristics: Use more courteous and longer greeting terms
Use high-pitched voice with equals or superiors
Use low-pitched voice with younger people
Hold both hands when greeting equals/superiors and prolong greeting
Have broader communicative and social competence
Use more experience in appropriate greeting selection
youth_characteristics: Limited social competence in greeting usage
Often use one hand for handshake, shorter duration
Influenced by Western culture, especially in cities
May not know when, how, why, and with whom to use certain terms despite knowing them
Usually criticized by adults for social mistakes in greeting usage
acquisition_progression: Socio-religious greetings like 'Thayu' taught from childhood
Cultural greetings like 'wakia maitu/awa' acquired in later stage
Children develop gradually a linguistic repertoire appropriate for all occasions
Time-specific or context-dependent greetings acquired later than general ones

male_characteristics: Use more standard and courteous code of greeting than females (contrast with Western cultures)
Less likely to use time-specific greetings
Cultural greetings express loyalty to Gikuyu tradition
Old males often use 'wakwa maitu or wanyua' and 'Thayu'
Old males hold both hands of equals/superiors and prolong greetings
Use same term to greet individuals or groups, males or females
female_characteristics: Use greetings much more frequently than men
Sometimes violate etiquette rules by responding with 'ĩĩ' instead of proper greeting
Use time-specific greetings more ('niatia ruciini', 'uhoro wa hwai-ini')
Middle-aged women use kinship-term greetings much more than middle-aged men
Educated young women tend to use foreign features ('hai', 'halo')
Women who marry men from higher social class use more prestigious/urban forms
Women achieve higher degree of intimacy through cultural relational terms and low-pitched tone
Women are more sincere in greetings though may use less formal/polite terms
Old females often use more courteous variants of greetings
Women's greetings may be influenced by husband's social status
gender_markers.frequency_differences: Men and women do not usually use different forms, but the frequency of forms may be different

greeting_initiation_rules: Smaller group greets larger group first
Walking person greets sitting person first
politeness_hierarchy: With professional title - Very high (shows respect, urbanity)
With kinship term - High when appropriate (shows solidarity/respect)
Niatia - Medium (neutral, less courteous than Thayu)
Casual health inquiry (Uga) - Low-medium (intimate/superior to subordinate)
First name only - Low (can be discourteous depending on relationship)
greeting_direction_politeness: In female-dominant society (Kiambu/Nyeri rural), more polite terms used to greet males
Females may be greeted with less courteous terms
Age and social status of greetee affect greeter's choice
Young people may not be greeted by old people (with greeting term)
Inferiors may be offered less courteous greeting than superiors
response_etiquette: Greeted person should reply with more courteous greeting or at least equal courtesy
Longer greeting = more polite and courteous
Response usually longer and more courteous than offered greeting

handshaking_situations.appropriate: Use greetings that accommodate handshaking (Thayu, Niatia, Nikwega)
handshaking_situations.inappropriate: Avoid 'muriega' when handshake expected - may signal relationship no longer close

group_situations.appropriate: Use 'muriega' when greeting large sitting group without individual handshakes
group_situations.cultural_rule: When there is big number of sitting people and no need to shake everyone's hand

work_situations.original_usage: 'Uhoro' or 'waigua atía' originally for greeting someone performing physical job
work_situations.current_usage: Now becoming widespread, used to greet anyone even sitting people

passing_by.traditional_obligation: Expected to cast greetings to everyone met in street whether known or not

formal_occasions.appropriate: Use 'Thayu' or greetings with professional titles
formal_occasions.shows: Knowledge of linguistic and social norms - seen as educated and civilized

social_competence.definition: Occurrence of greeting terms depends not on linguistic competence but on social competence
social_competence.importance: Choosing appropriate term shows speaker is knowledgeable of linguistic and social norms
social_competence.judgment: Proper use shows speaker is educated, civilized, and properly brought up
social_competence.youth_challenge: Young people might have acquired terms early but don't know when, how, why, and with whom to use them

handshaking.old_males_to_young: May use one hand, shorter duration
handshaking.females_young_males: Often use one hand, shorter duration
handshaking.inappropriate_combination: Using 'muriega' while shaking hands may indicate relationship no longer close

voice_characteristics.women_for_intimacy: Low-pitched tone to achieve higher degree of intimacy
voice_characteristics.general_importance: Tone of voice conveys social relationship and intent

facial_expression.requirement: Greeting without smile or appropriate mode may reveal hostility or lack of intimacy
facial_expression.importance: Non-verbal mode conveys pragmatic and sociocultural aspects of meaning
facial_expression.alignment: Non-verbal behavior must align with verbal greeting or it affects semantic relationship

paralinguistic_extralinguistic.revelation: May reveal one's age and knowledge about cultural and social greetings
paralinguistic_extralinguistic.importance: Essential components of proper greeting etiquette

cultural_notes: Greetings are essential oil for peaceful social relations and open communication
Gikuyus genuinely tend to oil wheels of social interaction by adhering to long-standing conventions
Done not merely to make life easy but out of respect, consideration, and affection for fellows
Expected to greet everyone met in street, whether known or unknown (traditional)
Modern young people in cities may only greet close acquaintances (Western influence)
Thayu is most polite greeting - greeting of Gikuyu (founder) and offspring
Forefathers ordered Gikuyu speakers to spread 'Thayu' greeting among themselves
Longer greetings are more polite and courteous
Response should be at least equal in courtesy to offered greeting
Greetings establish identity and affirm solidarity
Greetings are essential for establishment of interpersonal relationships
Greetings introduce business or social topic
Inquiries about health establish social contact - greeter may not really wish to know details
Conversants may inquire about each other's and relatives' health simply to establish social contact
Compliance with recognized social rules of etiquette is expected
Proper linguistic etiquette shows speaker is educated and civilized
Violation of greeting etiquette can cause social conflicts
Greetings encompass proper forms of address (relational or absolute)
Gikuyus often extend social titles beyond actual family to display courtesy or solidarity
Kikuyu is a tonal language - accent marks indicate high tones
Time-specific greetings may indicate age and sex
Cultural greetings express loyalty to Gikuyu traditions
Women tend to be more sincere in greetings though may use less formal terms
In parts of Africa whole languages may be evaluated in terms of greeting patterns
Greetings gain semantic meaning from culture - content is culture-specific
Face-to-face interaction in Gikuyu heavily dependent on proper greeting phraseology
Greetings serve to reestablish social relations, acknowledge status, guarantee safe passage
Greetings are rituals consisting of verbal and non-verbal forms
Greetings are important strategies for negotiation and control of social identity and relationships

for_non_native_speakers: Must understand both verbal and non-verbal rules of greeting usage
Semantic meaning comes from culture - content is culture-specific
Choose appropriate term based on: age, gender, status, intimacy level, context, time
Learning greetings shows social competence, not just linguistic competence
Foreigners may face difficulties using certain greeting terms appropriately
Must determine verbal and non-verbal rules for both greeter and greetee parts
Understanding context crucial - e.g., 'mwaigua atia' only appropriate for large sitting groups
acquisition_stages: Children taught socio-religious greetings like 'Thayu' from childhood
Cultural greetings like 'wakia maitu/awa' acquired in later stage of life
Children develop gradually a linguistic repertoire appropriate for all occasions
Time-specific and context-dependent greetings acquired later than general greetings
Young people and adolescents might know terms but not know when/how/why/with whom to use them
common_mistakes: Young people overgeneralizing 'nikwega' for all occasions regardless of appropriateness
Responding with less courteous greeting than offered
Females responding with just 'ĩĩ' instead of proper greeting (violates etiquette)
Young person calling elder by first name without permission
Not adapting greeting to time of day or social context
Greeting inappropriately for one's age/status relationship to addressee
Neglecting non-verbal components (smile, appropriate tone, handshake)
Using intimate greetings in formal situations or vice versa
success_indicators: Choosing greeting appropriate to social status, age, sex, closeness
Using greeting consonant with social situation
Reflecting social relationship between interlocutors through greeting choice
Proper use of verbal and non-verbal components together
Responding with equal or more courteous greeting
Showing respect through appropriate terms of address
Demonstrating cultural knowledge and social competence

greeting_types.function: Mark time of day, show awareness of context

social_functions: Serve as switch that opens or closes relations
Confirm human sociability and social order

for_youth_and_urban_raised: Sensitization programme on importance of linguistic etiquettes in greetings
Education on greetings unique to various settings and different times
Learning proper greeting usage as way of solving conflicts from social interactions
Understanding cultural significance beyond Western influence
Practicing appropriate greeting selection for different social situations
for_learners: Start with 'Thayu' as foundational most-polite greeting
Understand hierarchy rules (who greets whom first)
Practice both verbal and non-verbal components together
Learn kinship terms for showing respect and solidarity
Understand context-specific usage (work, groups, formal, intimate)
Study examples of appropriate and inappropriate usage
Develop social competence alongside linguistic competence
